item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't think much of this the first time I read it, probably not making connection well enough with the whole "he comes" sort of thing. Upon re-reading it with the author's explanation in tow, I value it much more than what I did the first time. Things that were unclear to me or just weird now all make sense, and the parts that were creepy the first time are now even more so. As TL333s mentioned, the final addendum is pretty creepy, and that was applicable both times. These text-based things that are able to bring down whole buildings can't take down whatever "he" is even when they're together, and their remains are, in terms of text-based beings, pretty horrifying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first time I read this (maybe last week?), I wasn't very moved by it. I felt very "meh" about the whole thing, neither liking nor disliking it. I came to the comments to see if anyone said anything that would make me feel differently, but nothing jumped out, and I didn't make it past the first page of them.

I did not vote.

I came back to it today, sort of by accident, and my second reading totally changed my mind. I don't know what was different this time, but it sparked my imagination in a way that just didn't happen the first time around. I ended up reading all of the comments, and saw that this version is actually the rewrite, but I haven't gone back and looked at the original version yet. I am excited to, though. I'm always curious about past iterations, especially when the current one has made my ears perk up and my whiskers point forward while my head tilts and I lean a little.

like this kitty
Very well deserved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forgive me for a 2-year necro, but the point of the countdown is that we, as a species, have already failed. When that countdown finishes, humanity is dead. There's no fixing it. We missed our chance. All we can do is wait.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the warehouse raid says "shown above" at one point, is there supposed to be a picture?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, I understand where you're going with it, but all too quickly, I found my eyes skipping down the article, looking for a payoff which never came for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it more until the foundation 'failed'. I mean in containment of SCP-231, SCP-231-1 through 6 the foundation failed to contain and each made a bigger bang or whatever a ████████████ event is. The last one, SCP-231-7 if the foundation fails is a XK end of the world class kaboom. I liked where 1374 became panicked and urgent but an article that ends in a countdown to the end of the world doesn't appeal nearly as much as one where the suspense of an upcoming failure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This rewrite is much better, downvote changed to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the only issue I had, too. Fix that and you're golden.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Funny, I thought of the kids' guardians.

-B is definitely Dad/Nanna Egbert, supportive to a fault
-C strikes me as sort of Striderish?
-D reminds me a bit of Rose's belief that her mom is being snarky at her
Which I guess leaves -A to be Jade's dead grandpa

And of course, the thing what's gonna kill us all is Lord English.

item.title: Re: Authorial Explanation Time!
item.content:encoded: Serpent's Hand doesn't really have a building other than the Library, and like hell is the Library going to let itself get constantly graffiti'd and have its books tampered with. Plus, the Library might not even be in their realm of possibility; who's to say how far these things can reach? Plus, to some outside viewers (i.e. -F), Serpent's Hand seems less like fighters than the CI does.

item.title: Re: Authorial Explanation Time!
item.content:encoded:
One tiny, tiny nitpick:
In light of the above, wouldn't F have also done a good job trying to work with the Serpent's Hand?

item.title: Authorial Explanation Time!
item.content:encoded:
I do apologize in advance if this ruins the article for you, but I really hope it gives some more insight into what exactly is happening here.

So here you go.
- hide

Each iteration of SCP-1374 is a different entity that communicates with our world through the writing. These entities are trying to talk to people/help them because they know that a separate, larger entity is approaching that is going to endanger both them and the SCPverse. As such, they turn to our realm in order to make sure we're prepared for the approach on our side, whatever that may mean. (Each chose the GoI they went after separately; three entities did not purposefully all choose the Foundation in order to work in tandem with each other)

  • -A is frustrated at the Foundation, but recognizes it as the most viable option at the time. It really tries to help them in quelling what is to come, but can't really physically interact with the world.
  • -B is an entity that wants to help the humans protect themselves rather than have the humans protect it. It is quite fond of the humans and dotes on them a lot, but also expects too much of them. It honestly thought the Foundation was ready for the containment breaches it unleashed.
  • -C is mostly pessimistic and skeptical of the abilities of the Foundation. Its logic is, "If they can't even handle little error due to slips of attention, how can they possibly handle the big thing that's coming?"
  • Now, -D is different in these regards. Instead of turning to the humans in order to help them fight against the thing that's coming, it is trying to get them ready to worship it.
  • -F is actually a group of entities rather than a single entity. It turns to the group that it thinks will be able to protect it. In this case, why the Chaos Insurgency, you might ask. In short, everyone else looks pretty bad. The Foundation doesn't exactly have the nicest treatment towards anomalies, the GOC is even worse, MC&D is a bunch of smug rich bastards, etc etc. On the other hand, the Chaos Insurgency is versatile and doesn't immediately reject anomalies as evil. It escapes from any situation, regroups, and tries again. In the entities' eyes, the CI are fighters and could possibly protect them.

Additionally, each has limited scope in what they can do in the world.

  • -A doesn't actually have much ability in the physical world, but what it does have is extended knowledge about the nature of what's coming that the other's do not.
  • -B is able to affect systems installed into the structure it is infecting
  • -C has access to the information of the group it targets
  • -D can at the very least physically affect the people in the building it has infected
  • -F is able to damage the building itself

In short, all of these entities are frantically trying to get prepared for the coming of the thing. The first incident, they heard concrete rumors that the thing was on its way, so they decided to test us.

We failed.

Then, they heard the footsteps of its approach and tried to speed up our "training" process1. Of course, all that did was put more strain on the Foundation. Finally, the beast was close enough to be seen, at the very least, and we still were not ready. So, the entities decided that it would be the better course of action to stop wasting their energy on us and start trying to face the monster themselves. Of course, they failed, and the countdown began.

Final note about the course of this plotline; think about what "failing" would entail. Would that mean that they all died, and that the coming horror is completely unimpeded now? Or does it mean that they all became allied with the thing and are going to help it when it comes? Or does it mean that the thing actually made more of itself out of them? I dunno, that's up to your own imagination.

Footnotes
1. And yes, those [REDACTED]s are supposed to imply that Foundationverse events and disasters occurred at the same time as this happened, implying the scope of the creature that's coming. And no, it was never meant to tie in to any actual real-world things.
- hide

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven't seen the old version, but this is certainly quite nice. I think you might have gone a wee bit overboard with the redactions, but considering the nature of this SCP it's at least understandable. Overall, certainly deserving of an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love SCPs that fuck with the Foundation. That this one includes the GOC and MCD makes it even better. I also like the idea of a textual Keter; given the methods by which -C can mess with containment procedures, I think it's warranted. That final addendum gave me chills. It's a major "oh shit" moment and a great way to end this. Long though this article may be, I was intrigued all the way through. Also, 1374-B is Fluttershy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Remember when I said I would write and add more to this next week when I wouldn't be busy with school?

I lied.

THE REWRITE IS NOW ACTIVE.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I originally didn't get it either. But then I realized that one of the instances screwed up royally ending up with an extreme containment breach, and if one instance was capable of doing that, imagine the stuff that would happen if they all fucked up royally at the same time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really like this one. There's just one small thing:

…why Keter? It doesn't seem explicitly threatening to anyone except the Founda-OH I GET IT.

Nevermind. Have my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The point of that one was to imply that SCP-1374-C had done something with the documentation of something that fucked everything up so badly that it was deemed necessary for redaction. In my personal mind, that included the spreading or exposure to some sort of infohazard. But in the end, I think you're right and will improve upon this when I work on this is the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only problem with this is this:

On the same day at Sector 15, [REDACTED]

Maybe there's a reference I don't get, but I think this redaction needs more context. Literally anything could be between those brackets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This probably won't make folks like it more, but I admit I suddenly thought of the Homestruck trolls when I thought about this SCP more.

I can especially hear the first one as Karkat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As stated above thread, I am likely to revamp this and haul it into maximum overdrive over the course of ~the next week, probably.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. Well, given the generally positive response thus far from the article, I don't think I'll take it down for editing. However, over the course of the next week I will have a lot more time for writing due to being on spring break; as such (since several authors I highly respect have spoken about it), I will likely work on expanding this into something even greater. No promises, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops, yep, that's definitely supposed to be blackboxed insteand of 1374. Thanks for catching that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was gonna either downvote this so hard or upvote this so hard but I decided with a downvote. In my opinion it's far too meta (in a bad way) to be an SCP. The entire time I read it all I could imagine was someone sitting at their computer, writing an SCP for this website.
I think the perspective that your SCP is coming from is either too omnipotent, or the omnipotence isn't explained well enough.

e. While writing this comment I kind of changed my mind and changed to an upvote. I kept the original comment just to let you know that perhaps I was just shocked at someone being so brave for an SCP, something I personally haven't seen in the year I've been here.

ee. Although I do personally still think that the knowledge behind this needs to be explained, because as I voiced earlier, your casual rebuttals of containment procedures etc. do just really remind me of what someone would post on the comments of an article.

eee. (I'm just re-reading this because I like it) from "Addendum-1374-Omega" and onwards I think seems a tad sloppy. Randomly introducing iterations of the SCP that you haven't mentioned before (SCP-1374-G - wtf are they?) as well as some sentence errors and randomly throwing in 71 missing SCPs. Seems as if you need to review the last bit a tad more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
transformation of sixty-three (63) personnel into instances of SCP-1374-G

Isn't that supposed to be SCP-████-G, or something like that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep. This could be executed to be one of those really long but good story SCPs, like 1425. Instead, it ends after the first chapter.

I'm generally not a fan of SCP Foundation-specific anomalies, so since this didn't blow me away, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If more people comment about this, I'll consider rewriting it in the future in a way that explores the concept further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What Rumetzen said. It's a whole lotta "coulda" without a a whole lotta "did".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eeeeh. It's a clever idea well executed, but there isn't enough payoff in my opinion. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Awww, das cute, you KILLED IT AND YOU HAVE NO IDEA WHAT IT ACTUALLY DOES! WHAT THE HELL WERE YOU THINKING, YOU BARELY KILLED IT LAST TIME, YOU THINK STUFF THAT CAN COME DO THAT ISN'T GONNA COME BACK READY? USES YOUR MINDS LOSERS."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Keep up the good work"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it in chat, and I like it here.

The idea is brilliant, and the execution is even better. My only complaint is that I honestly cannot imagine what the GOC one is saying, but that's probably because it's late.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kill destroy kill tacticool?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty interesting.

I have to wonder what the one with the GOC is telling them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, I knew you could pull it off Azzle. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the way this interacts with other GOI's.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Credit goes to Pixeltasim for
giving me the idea that they were preparing the Foundation for something bigger

And thanks to everyone in chat that looked over this
